content.text: How DeployIfNotExists Works

DeployIfNotExists (DINE) is the most complex Azure Policy effect. When a resource is created or updated and the policy condition matches, DINE checks whether a companion resource (a VM extension, a diagnostic setting, a private endpoint connection) already exists in a configuration that satisfies the existence condition. If the companion is absent or does not match, the policy engine triggers an ARM deployment using the template embedded in the policy definition. The deployment runs asynchronously — typically 5 to 15 minutes after the triggering resource is created — using a managed identity associated with the policy assignment.

attrs.alt: DeployIfNotExists two-phase flow: resource creation and async companion deployment

content.text: DINE Policy Definition Anatomy

A DINE policy definition's then.details block is significantly more complex than other effects. It specifies the companion resource type to check, a name or existenceCondition to evaluate, the role the managed identity needs, and the full ARM deployment template to execute when the companion is missing.
DINE Assignment and Managed Identity

A DINE policy assignment must include a managed identity because the ARM deployment template is executed by that identity, not the original requestor. The identity must be granted the role specified in roleDefinitionIds at the assignment scope. After creating the assignment, always grant the role before expecting any deployments to succeed.

# Create DINE assignment with system-assigned identity
az policy assignment create \
  --name deploy-law-agent-windows \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --policy $DINE_DEF_ID \
  --mi-system-assigned \
  --location eastus2 \
  --params '{"logAnalyticsWorkspaceId":{"value":"/subscriptions/11111111/resourceGroups/rg-platform/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/law-corp"}}'

# Get the managed identity object ID
IDENTITY_OID=$(az policy assignment show \
  --name deploy-law-agent-windows \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp \
  --query 'identity.principalId' -o tsv)

# Grant Contributor at the MG scope (needed to create VM extensions)
az role assignment create \
  --assignee $IDENTITY_OID \
  --role Contributor \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp

# Verify setup
az policy assignment show \
  --name deploy-law-agent-windows \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp \
  --query '{Display:displayName,Identity:identity.type,MI:identity.principalId}'

The DINE deployment fails silently with a 403 if the managed identity doesn't have the Contributor (or more specific) role at the right scope. Always verify the role assignment exists before creating the first remediation task. Check remediation deployment failures with az policy remediation deployment list.
Remediation Tasks for Existing VMs

VMs created before the DINE assignment was made will not have the extension. They appear as non-compliant (companion resource missing or existenceCondition not met). A remediation task runs the deployment template against each non-compliant resource using the assignment's managed identity.

# Check how many VMs are non-compliant (missing the extension)
az policy state list \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --filter "policyAssignmentName eq 'deploy-law-agent-windows' and complianceState eq 'NonCompliant'" \
  --query 'length(@)' -o tsv

# Create remediation task at MG scope
az policy remediation create \
  --name remediate-law-agent \
  --policy-assignment /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/deploy-law-agent-windows \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --resource-discovery-mode ReEvaluateCompliance

# Monitor remediation status
az policy remediation show \
  --name remediate-law-agent \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --query '{Status:provisioningState,Total:deploymentSummary.totalDeployments,Success:deploymentSummary.successfulDeployments,Failed:deploymentSummary.failedDeployments}'

# If some deployments fail, list the individual failures
az policy remediation deployment list \
  --name remediate-law-agent \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --query "[?provisioningState=='Failed'].{Resource:resourceId,Error:error.code}" \
  -o table

# Scope a remediation task to a single subscription (faster, smaller blast radius)
az policy remediation create \
  --name remediate-law-agent-sub \
  --policy-assignment /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/deploy-law-agent-windows \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --resource-discovery-mode ReEvaluateCompliance
Terraform: DINE Policy Assignment
Common DINE Failure Patterns
Failure
Symptom
Fix
Managed identity missing Contributor role
Remediation deployments show 403 errors in deployment list
Grant the role in roleDefinitionIds to the MI at the assignment scope
location not set on assignment with identity
Assignment creation fails: location required when identity is present
Add --location eastus2 to az policy assignment create, or location = in Terraform
existenceCondition too strict
Resource marked non-compliant and re-deployed even when extension exists
Verify existenceCondition matches actual extension provisioningState — use Succeeded not Creating
DINE deployment template uses wrong field() reference
Extension deployed with empty vmName or wrong location
Verify template parameters reference [field('name')] and [field('location')] correctly
Remediation task at wrong scope
Task finishes but non-compliant count unchanged
Assignment at MG scope needs remediation at MG scope or subscription scope — not resource group
Concurrent remediation tasks overload VMs
Multiple extensions being installed simultaneously on same VM
Use --resource-group to scope remediation tasks to batches rather than running MG-wide at once
DINE for Diagnostic Settings

One of the most common DINE patterns is automatically deploying diagnostic settings to resources. Every Azure resource that generates logs can have diagnostic settings that route logs to Log Analytics, Storage Accounts, or Event Hubs. Without DINE, teams manually create these settings or miss them entirely. With DINE, every new resource in scope receives a diagnostic setting automatically within minutes of creation.
existenceCondition Scoping

The existenceCondition tells the policy engine what properties the companion resource must have to consider the primary resource compliant. If the existenceCondition is missing (only the type is specified), then any companion resource of that type counts as compliant — even if it is pointing to the wrong workspace or has the wrong settings. Always specify enough existenceCondition criteria to ensure the companion resource is actually providing the required governance.

# Too permissive: any diagnostic setting makes the KV compliant (wrong workspace OK)
# existenceCondition: {} OR omitted
# Risk: existing diagnostic setting pointing to old/decommissioned workspace
# => policy won't re-deploy to correct workspace

# Correct: existenceCondition checks the specific workspace
# existenceCondition:
#   field: Microsoft.Insights/diagnosticSettings/workspaceId
#   equals: [parameters('logAnalyticsWorkspaceId')]
# => if workspace ID doesn't match, re-deploy with correct workspace

# Verify existenceCondition is evaluating correctly:
# Check compliance state — if Compliant but wrong workspace, condition is too permissive
KV_ID='/subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-prod/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/kv-prod'

# Check actual diagnostic settings on the Key Vault
az monitor diagnostic-settings list \
  --resource $KV_ID \
  --query '[].{Name:name,WorkspaceId:workspaceId}' \
  -o table

# Check compliance state for the DINE assignment
az policy state list \
  --resource $KV_ID \
  --filter "policyAssignmentName eq 'deploy-kv-diag'" \
  --query '[].{State:complianceState,Action:policyDefinitionAction}' \
  -o table

# If Compliant but diagnostic setting points to wrong workspace,
# the existenceCondition is too loose — tighten it to check workspaceId
Testing DINE Policies Before Wide Rollout

Because DINE deploys resources using a managed identity, testing in a sandbox subscription before assigning at MG scope is essential. The deployment template can reference incorrect fields or produce errors that only appear when the managed identity attempts the actual deployment. The recommended test sequence: assign at a single test subscription → create a test VM → wait 10 minutes → verify extension/companion appears → check remediation deployment list for errors.

# Test DINE policy in a sandbox subscription

# Step 1: Assign to sandbox subscription only
az policy assignment create \
  --name deploy-law-agent-test \
  --subscription $SANDBOX_SUB_ID \
  --policy $DINE_DEF_ID \
  --mi-system-assigned \
  --location eastus2 \
  --params '{"logAnalyticsWorkspaceId":{"value":"$LAW_ID"}}'

IDENTITY_OID=$(az policy assignment show \
  --name deploy-law-agent-test \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SANDBOX_SUB_ID \
  --query 'identity.principalId' -o tsv)

az role assignment create \
  --assignee $IDENTITY_OID --role Contributor \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SANDBOX_SUB_ID

# Step 2: Create a test VM without the extension
az vm create \
  --resource-group rg-test \
  --name vm-dine-test \
  --image Win2022Datacenter \
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --admin-password $VM_PASSWORD

# Step 3: Trigger compliance scan
az policy state trigger-scan --subscription $SANDBOX_SUB_ID

# Step 4: Wait 10 minutes, then check if extension was deployed
sleep 600
az vm extension list \
  --vm-name vm-dine-test \
  --resource-group rg-test \
  --query '[].{Name:name,Publisher:publisher,State:provisioningState}' \
  -o table

# Step 5: Check for deployment failures
az policy state list \
  --subscription $SANDBOX_SUB_ID \
  --filter "policyAssignmentName eq 'deploy-law-agent-test' and complianceState eq 'NonCompliant'" \
  --query '[].resourceId' -o tsv

# If no failures and extension is present, promote to MG scope assignment
Resource Graph: Monitoring DINE Compliance

// All VMs that are non-compliant for the DINE assignment (extension missing)
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant'
| where properties.policyDefinitionAction == 'deployIfNotExists'
| where properties.policyAssignmentName == 'deploy-law-agent-windows'
| project
    vmId = tostring(properties.resourceId),
    subscriptionId,
    resourceGroup = tostring(properties.resourceGroup),
    complianceState = tostring(properties.complianceState)
| order by subscriptionId asc, resourceGroup asc

// Count compliant vs non-compliant VMs by subscription
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where properties.policyAssignmentName == 'deploy-law-agent-windows'
| where properties.resourceType =~ 'microsoft.compute/virtualmachines'
| summarize
    Compliant = countif(properties.complianceState == 'Compliant'),
    NonCompliant = countif(properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant')
    by subscriptionId
| extend CompliancePercent = round(100.0 * Compliant / (Compliant + NonCompliant), 1)
| order by CompliancePercent asc

// Track compliance improvement over time after remediation
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where properties.policyAssignmentName == 'deploy-law-agent-windows'
| summarize
    NonCompliant = countif(properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant'),
    Compliant = countif(properties.complianceState == 'Compliant')
| project NonCompliant, Compliant,
    CompliancePct = round(100.0 * Compliant / (Compliant + NonCompliant), 1)
Summary

DeployIfNotExists evaluates whether a companion resource exists after the triggering resource is created. When the companion is absent or fails the existenceCondition check, the policy engine asynchronously triggers an ARM deployment using a managed identity. The deployment template is embedded in the policy definition and parameterized using field() references to values from the triggering resource. DINE is used for scenarios requiring a separate resource to be created as a side effect: VM extensions, diagnostic settings, private endpoint connections, role assignments, and other companion resources. Both the assignment and the managed identity's RBAC must be configured before any deployments will succeed — missing RBAC produces silent 403 failures in the remediation deployment list, not in the original resource creation.
